Unit-circle roots of (z+1)^6 - (z^3+1)^2

Let
  P(z) = (z+1)^6 - (z^3+1)^2.
How many distinct roots of P(z) lie on the unit circle |z|=1?

Solution.
Step 1 (unit_circle_trig_reduction, cosine_factorization, phase_cancellation)
Assume |z|=1, so z = e^{iĪø} for some real Īø (taken modulo 2\pi).
Use
  e^{iĪø}+1 = 2 cos(Īø/2)  \cdot  e^{iĪø/2}
and
  e^{i3Īø}+1 = 2 cos(3Īø/2)  \cdot  e^{i3Īø/2}.
Then
  (z+1)^6 = (2 cos(Īø/2) e^{iĪø/2})^6 = 2^6 cos^6(Īø/2)  \cdot  e^{i3Īø},
and
  (z^3+1)^2 = (2 cos(3Īø/2) e^{i3Īø/2})^2 = 2^2 cos^2(3Īø/2)  \cdot  e^{i3Īø}.
So P(z)=0 becomes
  2^6 cos^6(Īø/2) e^{i3Īø} - 2^2 cos^2(3Īø/2) e^{i3Īø} = 0.
Since e^{i3Īø}\neq0, cancel it and divide by 2^2:
  16 cos^6(Īø/2) = cos^2(3Īø/2).                        (1)

Step 2 (trig_identity_triple_angle, solve_polynomial_in_cos)
Let x = Īø/2 and c = cos x. Then (1) reads
  16 c^6 = cos^2(3x).
Use the triple-angle identity
  cos(3x) = 4c^3 - 3c.
Substitute into the equation:
  16 c^6 = (4c^3 - 3c)^2.

Step 3 (algebraic_simplification)
Expand and simplify:
  (4c^3 - 3c)^2 = 16c^6 - 24c^4 + 9c^2.
So
  16c^6 = 16c^6 - 24c^4 + 9c^2
  0 = -24c^4 + 9c^2
  0 = 3c^2(-8c^2 + 3).
Hence
  c = 0  or  c^2 = 3/8,
i.e.
  cos x \in { 0,  +\sqrt(3/8),  -\sqrt(3/8) }.                 (2)

Step 4 (solution_counting_on_interval)
As Īø ranges over [0,2\pi), we have x=Īø/2 ranging over [0,\pi).
On [0,\pi), cos x is strictly decreasing and hits each value in [-1,1] exactly once, so each value in (2)
corresponds to exactly one x in [0,\pi). Therefore there are exactly 3 values of x, hence exactly 3 values of Īø,
hence exactly 3 distinct points z=e^{iĪø} on the unit circle solving P(z)=0.

Answer: 3.

Uniqueness Test
1) (Reduction check) Re-derive (1) directly from P(e^{iĪø})=0 using e^{it}+1=2cos(t/2)e^{it/2} and confirm no step divides by 0 (e^{i3Īø} is never 0).
2) (Algebra check) Verify that 16c^6=(4c^3-3c)^2 simplifies to 3c^2(-8c^2+3)=0, so the only possible cos x values are 0, Ā±\sqrt(3/8).
3) (Counting check) Since x\in[0,\pi) and cos is strictly decreasing there, each of these three cos-values yields exactly one x; thus exactly three Īø and three distinct z on |z|=1.

3
